Name: information_theoretic_neural_compression
Title: Information-Theoretic Limits of Neural Network Compression: A Rate-Distortion Perspective
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that the fundamental limits of neural network compression can be characterized using rate-distortion theory, and that mutual information between the compressed and original models can serve as a proxy for compression quality.
Related Work: Recent works such as Spectral Quantization Routing and Geometric Signal Separation have explored training-free methods for precision allocation and signal separation in neural networks. However, these approaches lack a rigorous information-theoretic foundation. Our proposal builds upon these works by establishing a rate-distortion framework for neural network compression, providing a mathematical foundation for understanding the trade-offs between compression and distortion.
Abstract: Neural network compression is a crucial step in deploying large models in resource-constrained environments. However, existing methods often rely on heuristics or empirical methods, lacking a rigorous mathematical foundation. We propose a rate-distortion framework for neural network compression, where the goal is to minimize the number of bits required to represent the model while maintaining a specified level of distortion. We use mutual information between the compressed and original models as a proxy for compression quality and establish a rate-distortion curve that characterizes the fundamental limits of compression. Our approach provides a mathematical foundation for understanding the trade-offs between compression and distortion, enabling the development of more efficient and effective compression methods.
Experiments: Evaluate the rate-distortion curve for various neural network architectures and compression methods
Investigate the relationship between mutual information and compression quality
Develop and test new compression algorithms that optimize the rate-distortion trade-off
Risk Factors and Limitations: The complexity of the rate-distortion framework may limit its applicability to very large models
The choice of distortion measure may affect the results and require careful consideration
The development of new compression algorithms may require significant computational resources and expertise
